revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/76908861|岐須美美命]]"
revision.text:
'''岐須美美命'''（きすみみのみこと）は、[[Japanese mythology|日本神話]]に登場する人物・男神で、初代天皇[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]の子である。『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』中巻に登場し、『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』には登場しない<ref>{{Cite book|last=川口謙二|title=神々の系図|publisher=[[東京美術]]|isbn=978-4808700225|page=11|quote=『日本書紀』には岐須美美命の御名はなく、}}</ref>。 また、『古事記』でも事績に関する記載はなく、子孫に関する記載もない。
== 記紀での言及 == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。
=== 諸説 === 記紀の神武東征に関する伝承は出発地を「日向（ヒムカ）」としている。これを[[Hyūga Province|日向国]]（現在の[[Miyazaki Prefecture|宮崎県]]に相当）とみて、岐須美美命にまつわる地名も南九州のものと解釈する説がある{{Refn|日向国などの律令国が定められるのは記紀よりもあとの時代であるから、記紀の「ひむか」は単に方角を示すだけで、日向国（宮崎県）とは解釈しない説もある。|group="注"}}。たとえば母親の名である「アヒラ（阿比良・吾平津）」を[[Ōsumi Province|大隅国]]（現在の[[Kagoshima Prefecture|鹿児島県]]東部）姶羅郡（あいらのこおり）や[[Hyūga Province|日向国]]南部の油津（あぶらつ）（現在の[[Miyazaki Prefecture|宮崎県]][[Nichinan, Miyazaki|日南市]]）と関連付ける説がある。同様に出身地とみられる「アタ（阿多・吾田）」を[[Satsuma Province|薩摩国]]（鹿児島県西部）阿多郡や日向国南部の吾田（あがた）（現在の宮崎県日南市）に通ずるとみる説がある。
『日本書紀』第二「神代下」には、日向神話に登場する[[Hosuseri no Mikoto|火闌降命]]が「吾田君小橋等之本祖」とあり、「吾田君小橋」と『古事記』の「阿多之小椅君」を同一視するならば、岐須美美命の母親（アヒラヒメ・アヒラツヒメ）は九州にルーツがあるということになる。「小椅・小橋」を地名とみる説もある。
『日本書紀』にはタギシミミ1人の名があり、キスミミの名は『古事記』にあるのみで事績についての記載はなく、子孫の記載もない。『先代旧事本紀』においても同様である。『[[Fudoki|風土記]]』・『[[Man'yōshū|万葉集]]』・『[[Shinsen Shōjiroku|新撰姓氏録]]』にも登場しない。江戸時代の[[Kokugaku|国学者]][[Motoori Norinaga|本居宣長]]は『[[Kojiki-den|古事記伝]]』にて、本来は[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）の「多」の文字の脱漏により伝わった異称で、1人の人物を指していたのに2兄弟と誤認されて伝わったとの推察を示した。また、『先代旧事本紀』巻六「皇孫本紀」では「研耳命」の名を記す一方で、巻七「天皇本紀」では「研耳命」は登場せず、「神武天皇子四処」（手研耳命・神八井耳命・神渟名川耳尊・彦八井耳命）としている<ref name="茨田" />。
=== 兄のその後 === [[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]はのちに東方へ遠征し（神武東征）、[[Emperor Jimmu|橿原宮]]で初代天皇として即位する。このとき正妃としてヒメタタライスズヒメ（『古事記』ではヒメタタライスケヨリヒメ）を迎えて皇后とし、子を産ませた{{Refn|『日本書紀』には2人の皇子の名（[[神八井命]]・[[神渟名川耳尊]]）が、『古事記』には3人の皇子の名（[[日子八井命]]・[[神八井耳命]]・[[綏靖天皇|神沼河耳命]]）がある。|group="注"}}。これにより、アヒラヒメ（アヒラツヒメ）が産んだタギシミミとキスミミは庶子の立場となった。神武天皇が崩御すると、庶長子であるタギシミミはヒメタタライスズヒメが産んだ異母弟たちの殺害を目論むが事前に露呈し、逆に討たれた（タギシミミの反逆）。こののち、ヒメタタライスズヒメが産んだ末弟の[[Emperor Suizei|神渟名川耳尊]]が2代天皇[[Emperor Suizei|綏靖天皇]]として即位する。
== 脚注 ==
=== 注釈 === <references group="注" />
=== 出典 === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref>
*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>
*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}
=== 書誌情報 ===
* 『日本神名辞典』,神社新報社,1994,1995（第2版）,ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * 『日本古代神祇事典』,吉田和典／編,中日出版社,2000,ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * 『日本の神仏の辞典』,大島健彦・薗田稔・圭室文雄・山本節／編,大修館書店,2001,ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * 『日本の神様読み解き事典』,川口謙二／編著,柏書房,1999,2009（第9刷）,ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * 『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』,吉田邦博／著,学研パブリッシング,2015,ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * 『図説 歴代天皇紀』,水戸部正男／編著,肥後和男・赤木志津子・福地重孝／著,[[Akita Shoten|秋田書店]],1989,ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * 『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』（別冊歴史読本24 第27巻29号・通巻618）,佐藤實／編,新人物往来社,2002 * 『日向国史 古代史』喜田貞吉／著,東洋堂,1943
== 関連項目 ==

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。

『日本書紀』第二「神代下」には、日向神話に登場する[[Hosuseri no Mikoto|火闌降命]]が「吾田君小橋等之本祖」とあり、「吾田君小橋」と『古事記』の「阿多之小椅君」を同一視するならば、岐須美美命の母親（アヒラヒメ・アヒラツヒメ）は九州にルーツがあるということになる。「小椅・小橋」を地名とみる説もある。
『日本書紀』にはタギシミミ1人の名があり、キスミミの名は『古事記』にあるのみで事績についての記載はなく、子孫の記載もない。『先代旧事本紀』においても同様である。『[[Fudoki|風土記]]』・『[[Man'yōshū|万葉集]]』・『[[Shinsen Shōjiroku|新撰姓氏録]]』にも登場しない。江戸時代の[[Kokugaku|国学者]][[Motoori Norinaga|本居宣長]]は『[[Kojiki-den|古事記伝]]』にて、本来は[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）の「多」の文字の脱漏により伝わった異称で、1人の人物を指していたのに2兄弟と誤認されて伝わったとの推察を示した。また、『先代旧事本紀』巻六「皇孫本紀」では「研耳命」の名を記す一方で、巻七「天皇本紀」では「研耳命」は登場せず、「神武天皇子四処」（手研耳命・神八井耳命・神渟名川耳尊・彦八井耳命）としている<ref name="茨田" />。

== 関連項目 ==

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== 兄のその後 === [[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]はのちに東方へ遠征し（神武東征）、[[Emperor Jimmu|橿原宮]]で初代天皇として即位する。このとき正妃としてヒメタタライスズヒメ（『古事記』ではヒメタタライスケヨリヒメ）を迎えて皇后とし、子を産ませた{{Refn|『日本書紀』には2人の皇子の名（[[神八井命]]・[[神渟名川耳尊]]）が、『古事記』には3人の皇子の名（[[日子八井命]]・[[神八井耳命]]・[[綏靖天皇|神沼河耳命]]）がある。|group="注"}}。これにより、アヒラヒメ（アヒラツヒメ）が産んだタギシミミとキスミミは庶子の立場となった。神武天皇が崩御すると、庶長子であるタギシミミはヒメタタライスズヒメが産んだ異母弟たちの殺害を目論むが事前に露呈し、逆に討たれた（タギシミミの反逆）。こののち、ヒメタタライスズヒメが産んだ末弟の[[Emperor Suizei|神渟名川耳尊]]が2代天皇[[Emperor Suizei|綏靖天皇]]として即位する。

== 関連項目 ==

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== After his brother === [Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].

=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].

== 関連項目 ==

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].

He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |故、坐日向時、娶阿多之小椅君妹・名阿比良比売生子、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]、次'''岐須美美命'''、二柱坐也。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]が）「日向」（ヒムカ）にいた頃、「阿多」（アタ）の「小椅君」の妹で名を阿比良比売（アヒラヒメ）を娶り、生まれた子が、[[Tagishimimi|多芸志美美命]]（タギシミミノミコト）、岐須美美命（キスミミノミコト）の2柱である。 |} 一方『日本書紀』巻三「神武天皇紀」には、大筋で同じ内容となる次のような一文がある。しかしこちらには子の名前は「タギシミミ」のみ記されていて、岐須美美命に相当する人物の言及がない。 {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |長而娶日向国吾田邑吾平津媛、為妃、生手研耳命。 |- |（大意）（[[Emperor Jimmu|神武天皇]]は）長じて「日向（ヒムカ）国」の「吾田（アタ）邑」の吾平津媛（アヒラツヒメ）を娶って妻とし、[[Tagishimimi|手研耳命]]（タギシミミノミコト）が生まれた。 |} このほか『[[Kujiki|先代旧事本紀]]』巻六「皇孫本紀」には神武天皇と吾平津媛の2人の子として「手研耳命」と「研耳命」（キシミミノミコト）の名が登場する。が、巻七「天皇本紀」には登場しない。
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].

*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>

*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten|Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki|Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{Refn There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture). |group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province|Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki|Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto)<ref name="Ibarada" />.
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== 脚注 ==
=== 出典 === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref>
*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>
*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten|Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name="Ibarada" />
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== 脚注 ==

*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>

*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}

revision.comment: ActivelyDisinterested moved page [[Kisumimi]] to [[Draft:Kisumimi]]: Incomplete translation.
revision.text:
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{#ref:There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name="Ibarada" />
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== 脚注 ==

*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref>
*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>
*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}

There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{#ref:There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name="Ibarada" />
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== Footnotes ==
=== Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>

*<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref>
*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>
*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}

revision.comment: SiliconProphet moved page [[Draft:Kisumimi]] to [[Kisumimi]]: completed translation
revision.text:
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{#ref:There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name="Ibarada" />
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). |group="Note"}} As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== Footnotes ==

*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>

He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{#ref:There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}}
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== Footnotes ==
=== Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref>
*<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref>
*<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref>
*<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref>
*<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>
*<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref>}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

revision.comment: /* Source */ Commented out refs not in use, to solve cite errors
revision.text:
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}}
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]].
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}}
=== Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> <!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref> Not in use-->
}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

[[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
