{{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jinmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor". === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province. The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''Kamuyaimimi'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

{{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married Abira-hime, the younger sister of [[Ata]], whose name was Abira-hime, and their children were [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-mikoto]] and Kisumi-no-mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jinmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor". === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province. The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jinmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jinmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''[[Kamuyaimimi]]'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==

{{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimi-no-mikoto]] and [[Kisumi-no-mikoto]]. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor". === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province. The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{CN|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==

== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Source === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

{{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor". === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province. The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{Citation needed|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''[[Kamuyaimimi]]'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 {{Uncategorized|date=November 2021}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto''' is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto. |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto. {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi. |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor". === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province. The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime Ahiratsu-hime) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).{{Citation needed|date=October 2021}} === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed Himetataraisuhime (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01=[[Himetataraisuzu-hime]]|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''[[Kamuyaimimi]]'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''[[Kamuyaimimi]]'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)" is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]""Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei". He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]]."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi" |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu" |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)". However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu""Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}" The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu". There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name."Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei" In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03=[[Ahiratsu-hime]]}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''[[Kamuyaimimi]]'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi" |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |This is the name of a woman who was married to a man named Gisumimi."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu" |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)". However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu""Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}" The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu". There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name."Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei" In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03={{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''{{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the Nihon Shoki''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree|01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03={{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}}} {{Familytree|||,|-|(||||||!|}} {{Familytree|01|02||||03|01='''{{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]}} {{Family tree/end}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|bdp}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220112225748|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)" is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]""Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei". He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]]."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi" |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu" |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)". However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu""Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}" The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu". There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name."Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei" In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east (Jimmu Tōkyoku) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==

After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
