== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|bdp}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220112225748|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)" is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]""Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei". He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]]."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi" |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu" |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)". However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu""Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}" The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu". There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name."Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei" In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the [[Kojiki]]''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree| | |01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03={{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}}} {{Familytree| |,|-|-|v|-|^|.|||||)|-|-|.|}} {{Familytree|Hi| |01|02||03| |Ki|01='''{{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]|Hi=[[Hikoyai]]*|Ki={{Interlanguage link|Kisumimi|lt=|ja|岐須美美命}}*}} {{Family tree/end}} * = not in the [[Nihon Shoki]]

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|bdp}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220112225748|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}} '''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)" is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]""Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei". He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]]."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}} and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki. There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants. == References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto."Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)""Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi" |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto."Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]" {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu" |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)". However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province."Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu""Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}" The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu". There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name."Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei" In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothersYoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society === After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastardsKojiki and '''''a''''' book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei|Suiyasu]]. == Genealogy ==
'''A genealogical tree based on the [[Kojiki]]''' {{Family tree/start}} {{Familytree| | |01|~|y|~|02|~|y|~|03|01={{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}|02=[[Jimmu]]|03={{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}}} {{Familytree| |,|-|-|v|-|^|.|||||)|-|-|.|}} {{Familytree|Hi| |01|02||03| |Ki|01='''{{Interlanguage link|Kamuyaimimi|ja|神八井耳命}}'''|02=[[Emperor Suizei]]|03=[[Tagishimimi]]|Hi={{Interlanguage link|Hikoyai|ja|日子八井命}}*|Ki={{Interlanguage link|Kisumimi|lt=|ja|岐須美美命}}*}} {{Family tree/end}} * = not in the [[Nihon Shoki]]
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs= }} === Bibliographic information === * "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943 [[:Category:Japanese deities]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2022}}

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese Prince}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|bdp}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220112225748|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220112151719|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Kisumimi]] before draftification: *[[Draft:Kisumimi]] *[[Kisumimi-no-Mikoto]] --> {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}}
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref> is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki.
There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref>. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref>. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref>. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].
== Genealogy ==
{{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> <!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref> Not in use-->
}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

{{Short description|Character in Japanese mythology }}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Kisumimi]] before draftification: *[[Draft:Kisumimi]] *[[Kisumimi-no-Mikoto]] --> {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}}
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref> is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]]<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>. He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki.
There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref>. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime {{Interlanguage link|Ahiratsu-hime|ja|吾平津媛}}) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref>. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed {{Interlanguage link|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=|ja|ヒメタタライスズヒメ}}(Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref>. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].

== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> <!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->

}}
=== Bibliographic information ===

However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref>. However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress [[Ahiratsu-hime]]"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime [[Ahiratsu-hime]]) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref>. There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>. The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]](Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref>. When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].
== Genealogy ==
{{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> <!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代天皇紀-神武">『図説 歴代天皇紀』p37-41「神武天皇」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="歴代皇后-神武">『歴代皇后人物系譜総覧』,p26-27「初代 神武天皇 皇后 媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="読み解き事典-岐須美美命">『日本の神様読み解き事典』p118「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="学研2015">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本人名大辞典＋">『デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus』,[[講談社]],2015,「媛蹈韛五十鈴媛命」[https://kotobank.jp/word/岐須美美命-1069200 コトバンク版] 2018年8月11日閲覧。</ref> Not in use-->
}}
=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

{{Short description|Character in Japanese mythology }}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Kisumimi]] before draftification: *[[Draft:Kisumimi]] *[[Kisumimi-no-Mikoto]] --> {{More citations needed|date=October 2021|talk=Untranslated refs in the reflist}}
'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref> is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref> He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki.
There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu.<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref> However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the Jimmu's eastern expedition in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress [[Ahiratsu-hime]]"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime [[Ahiratsu-hime]]) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers.<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]](Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards.<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref> When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].
== Genealogy ==
{{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{notelist}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist|30em|refs=<!-- --> <!-- Not in use *<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">『日本神名辞典』p151「岐須美美命」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="古代神祇-きすみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p341「きすみみのみこと（岐須美美命）」</ref> *<ref name="古代神祇-きしみみ">『日本古代神祇事典』p340「きしみみのみこと（研耳命）」</ref> Not in use-->
<!-- Not in use *<ref name="神仏の辞典-きすみみ">『日本の神仏の辞典』p400「きすみみのみこと【岐須美美命】」</ref> Not in use-->

=== Bibliographic information ===
* "Dictionary of Japanese Divine Names", Jinja-Shimpo-Sha, 1994, 1995 (2nd edition), ISBN 4-915265-66-8 * "Dictionary of Japan's Ancient Shinto Gods", Kazunori Yoshida/editor, Chunichi Press, 2000, ISBN 4-88519-158-0 * "Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha", Takehiko Oshima, Minoru Sonoda, Fumio Keimuro, Setsu Yamamoto (eds.), Taishukan Shoten, 2001, ISBN 4-469-01268-8 * "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1 * "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2 * "Illustrated Chronicle of the Rekiyo Emperors", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, [[Akita Shoten]], 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8 * "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomihon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), edited by Minoru Sato, Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002 * "The History of Hyuga Province: An Ancient History" by Sadakichi Kida, Toyo-do, 1943

There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu.<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref> However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the [[Jimmu's eastern expedition]] in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress [[Ahiratsu-hime]]"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime [[Ahiratsu-hime]]) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers.<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At this time, he welcomed [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]](Himetataraisukeyorihime in the [[Kojiki]]) as his consort and made her his empress, and had her bear his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards.<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref> When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref> is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref> He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki.
There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu.<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref> However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>

The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime [[Ahiratsu-hime]]) has her roots in Kyushu. In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers.<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>

[[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards.<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref> When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second Emperor [[Emperor Suizei]].

'''Kisumimi-no-mikoto'''<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref> is a character in [[Japanese mythology]], a male deity and the son of the first Emperor [[Emperor Jimmu|Jimmu]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref> He appears in the middle volume of [[Kojiki]], but not in [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Kenji Kawaguchi|title=Genealogy of the Gods|publisher=[[Tokyo Bijutsu]]|isbn=978-4808700225| page=11|quote=There is no mention of Kisumimi-no-Mikoto in the Nihon Shoki,}}</ref> and the name is not mentioned in the Kojiki.
There is also no mention of his achievements in the Kojiki, and no mention of his descendants.
== References in Chronicles == In the "Record of Jimmu" in the middle volume of the "Kojiki," there is a sentence as follows.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |Therefore, when sitting in the sun, he took to wife the sister of Ata-no-Koji-kun, the son of Abirahime, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]], and the next, '''Gisumi-no-Mikoto''', the two pillars. |-. |When [[Emperor Jimmu]] was in Hyuga, he married the sister of Ata's second son, Abira-hime, and they had two sons, [[Tagishimimi|Tagishimimi-no-Mikoto]] and Kisumimi-no-Mikoto.<ref name=":0">"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p341 "Kisumi no Mikoto (Kisumi Mimei)"</ref><ref>"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p151 "Kismimi Kisumimi"</ref> |} On the other hand, in "The Chronicles of Emperor Jimmu," vol. 3 of the Nihon Shoki, there is a sentence that is largely the same as the following. However, in this text, only the name of the child is given as "Tagishimi," and there is no mention of the person who corresponds to Gisumi-no-mikoto.<ref name=":1">"Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Buddha" p400 "Kisumi no Mikoto [Kisumi Mimei]"</ref> {| style="background-color:WhiteSmoke;padding: 10px;" |The Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province. （Emperor Jinmu took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in Hyuga Province, and Tagishimimino-no-Mikoto was born.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> |-This is the first time I've seen it. |- ([[Emperor Jimmu]]) took to wife Ahiratsuhime of Ata-eup in the Land of Hyuga, and they had [[Tagishimimi|Tegashimimino-no-mikoto]]. |} In addition to this, in [[Kujiki|Sekaiyo Kyu-jihonki]], Volume 6, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", the names "Tagishimimi-no-mikoto" and "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" appear as the two sons of Emperor Jimmu and Princess Ohirazu.<ref>"Ancient Japanese Encyclopedia" p340 "Kismimi no Mikoto (Kimimi no Mikoto)"</ref> However, they do not appear in Volume 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperor".<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== Various theories === The legend of the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] in the Chronicles of Japan gives the starting point as "Himuka" (Hyuga). There is a theory that this is [[Hyūga Province]] (equivalent to present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]), and that the place names associated with Kisumimikonomikoto are also from southern Kyushu {{efn|There is also a theory that "Himuka" in Chronicles merely indicates a direction and is not interpreted as Hyuga Province (Miyazaki Prefecture).|group="note"}} For example, the mother's name "Ahira" is used for Aira no Koori in [[Ōsumi Province|Osumi Province]] (present-day eastern [[Kagoshima Prefecture]]) and Aburatsu in southern [[Hyūga Province|Hyuga Province]] (present-day [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]). There is also a theory that he is related to [[Nichinan, Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[Nichinan City]]. Similarly, there is a theory that Ata (Ata, Agata), which is thought to be his birthplace, is related to Ata-gun in [[Satsuma Province]] (western Kagoshima Prefecture) and Agata (present-day Nichinan City, Miyazaki Prefecture) in southern Hyuga Province.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref><ref>"Overview of the Genealogy of Empresses of All Time", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref><ref>Kida, p255-257 "Empress [[Ahiratsu-hime]]"</ref>
The second book of the Chronicles of Japan, "Shindaishita," states that [[Hosuseri no Mikoto|Higayasu-no-mikoto]], who appears in the Hyuga mythology, is the "main ancestor of Agata-kun Kobashi, etc." If we equate "Agata-kun Kobashi" with "Ata-no-Koji-kun" in the Kojiki, then the mother of Kisumi-no-mikoto (Ahira-hime [[Ahiratsu-hime]]) has her roots in Kyushu. If we equate "Ata no Kochair-kun" with "Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime," then the mother of Kisumi no Mikoto (Ahirahime Ahiratsuhime) has roots in Kyushu.<ref name=":3">"Illustrated Emperor Jimmu" p37-41 "Emperor Jimmu"</ref> There is a theory that "Kobashi" is a place name.<ref name=":2">"Japanese God Reading Dictionary" p118 "Kisumi Mimei"</ref>
In the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), the name of Tagishimi is mentioned only in the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters), but there is no mention of his achievements or descendants. The same is true in the "Old Chronicle of the First Age". The name does not appear in [[Fudoki]], [[Man'yōshū]], or [[Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsengumi Roku]]. In [[Kojiki-den]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], an Edo-period [[Kokugaku]] [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of [[Kokugaku]], was given the name Tagishimimi in [[Kojiki-den]]. It is believed that the word "Tagishimi" was used to refer to one person, but was misidentified as two brothers.<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=日本人名大辞典+Plus|first=デジタル版|title=岐須美美命とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B2%90%E9%A0%88%E7%BE%8E%E7%BE%8E%E5%91%BD-1069200|access-date=2022-01-12|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> The name "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" is mentioned in the "Sachiyo Kyu-jihonki", Vol. 6, "The Chronicles of the Imperial Grandchildren", while "Kenmimi-no-mikoto" does not appear in Vol. 7, "The Chronicles of the Emperors", which refers to "the four sons of Emperor Jimmu" (Tekenmimi-no-mikoto, Jinpaimimi-no-mikoto, Jingyanagawamimi-no-mikoto, and Hikoimimi-no-mikoto).<ref name=":4">Yoshii "The Story of Ibarata Ren's Ancestor Tradition and Ibarata Tsutsumi Construction" (Manyo No. 71, P1-p21, 1969) Manyo Society</ref>
=== After his brother === [[Emperor Jimmu]] later made an expedition to the east ([[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu Tōkyoku]]) and ascended to the throne as the first emperor at [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s Kashihara Palace. At that time, he welcomed [[Himetataraisuzu-hime]] (Himetataraisukeyorihime in the ''[[Kojiki]]'') as his consort and made her his empress. She bore his children{{Efn|In the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), the names of two princes are given (Kannaiyai-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto), and in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), the names of three princes are given (Hikoyai-no-mikoto, Kannaiyamimi-no-mikoto and Kannu-nagawamimi-no-mikoto).}}. [[Mimi-no-Mikoto]], [[Emperor Sui Yasushi|Kannuma Kawamimi-no-Mikoto]]). As a result, Tagishimi and Kisumimi, sired by Ahirahime (Ahiratsuhime), took the position of bastards.<ref>Kojiki and <sup>'''''a'''''</sup> book about Japanese gods" p90-91 "The story of Isukeyorihime</ref> When Emperor Jimmu collapsed, Tagishimi, the bastard son, planned to kill his half-brothers born to Himetathalaisuhime, but he was exposed before he could do so and was defeated (Tagishimi's treason). After this, the youngest brother [[Emperor Suizei|Shingyanagawamimi]], born to Himetataraisuhime, ascended the throne as the second emperor, [[Emperor Suizei]].
